coredata.dc:title: Phosphate-triggered ratiometric multicolor immunosensor based on nanobody-alkaline phosphatase fusion protein for sensitive detection of fenitrothion
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Min-Ling
Zeng, Xi
Deng, Hao
Wang, Yu
Zhang, Yi-Feng
Shen, Yu-Dong
Luo, Lin
Wang, Hong
Chen, Zi-Jian
Xu, Zhen-Lin
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor immunosensor is a promising tool for on-site rapid analysis by naked eye without bulky instruments. Herein, a multicolor immunosensor employed a specific nanobody-alkaline phosphatase fusion protein (VHHjd8-ALP) for biosensing of fenitrothion was developed. After one-step indirect competitive immunoassay, the binding VHHjd8-ALP in microplate catalyzed ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) into ascorbic acid, which propose dual readout including multicolor ratiometric visible immunoassay (MRVIA) and multicolor ratiometric fluoroimmunoassay (MRFIA). For MRVIA, K3[Fe(CN)6] and Fe3+ was employed for multicolor generation. The substrate AAP can chelate with Fe3+ to form orange AAP-Fe3+ complex. When ALP catalyzed AAP into ascorbic acid, the latter can reduced K3[Fe(CN)6] into K4[Fe(CN)6], which reacted with Fe3+ to produced Prussian blue via self-assembly. A visible color transition from orange to blue against fenitrothion was obtained. For MRFIA, the generated ascorbic acid etched MnO2 nanosheet to control the concentration of Na2SO3, which can convert 9-anthraldehyde into sulfuretted anthracene accompanied by a fluorescent color transition from green to blue. These two methods showed visible multicolor transition against fenitrothion for naked-eye analysis. The recovery and blind samples test verified good accuracy and practicability, which can be used for rapid screening of fenitrothion by a naked-eye semiquantitative analysis mode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor immunosensor
Ratiometric assay
Nanobody-alkaline phosphase fusion
Fenitrothion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development of China ( 2019YFE0116600 ), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation , China ( 2021A1515110513 ), the Science and Technology Foundation of Guangdong Province , China ( 2018A050506072 ) and the Key Project of the Guangdong Provincial High School , China ( 2019KJDXM002 ).

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development of China (2019YFE0116600), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation, China (2021A1515110513), the Science and Technology Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (2018A050506072) and the Key Project of the Guangdong Provincial High School, China (2019KJDXM002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of MRVIA. In the presence of ALP, AAP was converted into ascorbic acid, the latter reduced K3[Fe(CN)6] into K4[Fe(CN)6], which reacted with Fe3+ to generate Prussian blue via self-assembly. In contrast, orange AAP-Fe3+ complex was formed in the absence of ALP.

(A) The transmission electron microscope image, (B) HAADF, and (C) energy dispersive spectrometer image of Prussian blue particle; (D) The energy dispersive spectrometer spectrum of Prussian blue particle; (E) The spectrum of relevant compounds for MRVIA development, a: Fe3+; b: K3[Fe(CN)6]; c: K3[Fe(CN)6]+Fe3+; d: AAP+Fe3+; e: K4[Fe(CN)6]+Fe3+; f: ALP+AAP+ K3[Fe(CN)6]+Fe3+.

(A) The development of MRVIA using indirect competitive strategy. As the fenitrothion decreased, the AAP-Fe3+ was converted into Prussian blue, accompanied by the color transition from orange to blue; (B) The spectrum and photograph of the developed MRVIA; (C) Calibration curve of MRVIA.

(A) The color sensing of 9-anthraldehyde to Na2SO3. The fluorescence was excited by handle ultraviolet equipment (excitation: 365 nm); (B) The emission of sulfuretted anthracene (line 1) and 9-anthraldehyde (line 2) with the excitation of 365 nm; (C) The schematic diagram of MRFIA. Na2SO3 can converted 9-anthraldehyde into sulfuretted anthracene. Na2SO3 concentration can be controlled by MnO2 via oxidation, the latter can be etched by ascorbic acid. The present ALP can finally regulate the conversion through control the generation of ascorbic acid.
The pH optimizing for (A) 9-anthraldehyde and (B) sulfuretted anthracene; The (C) Na2SO3 and (D) MnO2 nanosheet concentration optimizing. The red squares indicated the optimized condition.

(A) The development of MRFIA using indirect competitive strategy. The absent of fenitrothion resulted in the generation of sulfuretted anthracene, accompanied by the fluorescent color transition from green to blue; (B) The spectrum and photograph of the developed MRFIA; (C) Calibration curve of MRFIA. The I520 was measured was mean fluorescence intensity of the wells in the absence of fenitrothion. I420 was mean fluorescence intensity of the wells in the presence of certain concentration of fenitrothion.
Recoveries of fenitrothion from spiked samples by MRFIA, MRVIA, and GC-MS/MS (n = 3) a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Real sample test by MRVIA, MRFIA, and GC-MS/MS (n = 3) a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: For one concentration, three samples were spiked and determined by MRVIA, MRFIA, and GC-MS/MS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of immunosensors for fenitrothion analysis in buffer and real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a

head.ce:title: Phosphate-triggered ratiometric multicolor immunosensor based on nanobody-alkaline phosphatase fusion protein for sensitive detection of fenitrothion

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Tropical Fruit and Vegetable Cold-chain of Hainan Province / Institute of Agro-products Processing and Design, Hainan Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Haikou, 570100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor immunosensor is a promising tool for on-site rapid analysis by naked eye without bulky instruments. Herein, a multicolor immunosensor employed a specific nanobody-alkaline phosphatase fusion protein (VHHjd8-ALP) for biosensing of fenitrothion was developed. After one-step indirect competitive immunoassay, the binding VHHjd8-ALP in microplate catalyzed ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) into ascorbic acid, which propose dual readout including multicolor ratiometric visible immunoassay (MRVIA) and multicolor ratiometric fluoroimmunoassay (MRFIA). For MRVIA, K3[Fe(CN)6] and Fe3+ was employed for multicolor generation. The substrate AAP can chelate with Fe3+ to form orange AAP-Fe3+ complex. When ALP catalyzed AAP into ascorbic acid, the latter can reduced K3[Fe(CN)6] into K4[Fe(CN)6], which reacted with Fe3+ to produced Prussian blue via self-assembly. A visible color transition from orange to blue against fenitrothion was obtained. For MRFIA, the generated ascorbic acid etched MnO2 nanosheet to control the concentration of Na2SO3, which can convert 9-anthraldehyde into sulfuretted anthracene accompanied by a fluorescent color transition from green to blue. These two methods showed visible multicolor transition against fenitrothion for naked-eye analysis. The recovery and blind samples test verified good accuracy and practicability, which can be used for rapid screening of fenitrothion by a naked-eye semiquantitative analysis mode.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The improper and excessive usage of pesticides may cause their residues in agricultural products, which result in health risks to human beings. Fenitrothion is a broad-spectrum organophosphate insecticide widely used to prevent agricultural products from insect pests, which however have adverse effects on humans [1,2]. Strict maximum residue limits (MRLs) in vegetables and fruits for fenitrothion have been set up by many countries and organizations, including China (0.5 mg/kg) [3], European Union (0.01 mg/kg) [4], USA (3.0 mg/kg) [5], and Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO, 0.5 mg/kg) [6]. For pesticide monitoring, immunoassay is a powerful tool owing to its rapidity, sensitivity, and easy-to-use [7–9]. However, conventional microplate-based immunoassay requires bulky readers, which are unsuitable for on-site analysis. As an alternative, multicolor immunosensors are more promising because the readers can be replaced by naked eye and the accuracy can be improved if color changes are related to target concentration [10,11].

For the development of multicolor immunosensors, precious-metal-based (including gold and silver) nanomaterials have been widely employed [12–14]. However, some drawbacks have limited their application, which includes: (i) High-cost of precious metal materials; (ii) Time-consuming caused by the screening of local surface plasmon resonance peak; (iii) Low absorbance and sensitivity caused by blue-shift [15–17]. Conversely, multicolor immunosensor based on color transition of the ratiometric change of two constant wavelength signals, including absorbance or fluorescence [18–20], is more straightforward and time-saving than the reported precious-metal material-based multicolor immunosensors.

In this study, the alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was employed as a signal probe to develop ratiometric multicolor immunosensors. For ALP labeling, chemical conjugation is often used but showed drawbacks of complex procedures, prolonged reaction periods, and significant batch errors [21]. To overcome this shortcoming, an anti-fenitrothion nanobody-ALP fusion protein (VHHjd8-ALP) was produced by using genetic sequence construction and soluble expression. After one-step indirect competitive immunoassay, the binding VHHjd8-ALP in microplate catalyzed substrate ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) into ascorbic acid for the development of dual readout multicolor immunosensors including multicolor ratiometric visible immunoassay (MRVIA) and multicolor ratiometric fluoroimmunoassay (MRFIA). For MRVIA, Fe3+ and K3[Fe(CN)6] were employed for the color sensing to AAP and ascorbic acid. The unreacted AAP can chelate Fe3+ to form the orange AAP-Fe3+ complex. As the converting of AAP to ascorbic acid, the latter can reduced K3[Fe(CN)6] into K4[Fe(CN)6] that reacted with Fe3+ to form Prussian blue through self-assembly. It was expected that a visible color transition from orange to blue against fenitrothion can be achieved. For MRFIA, 9-anthraldehyde was used as a fluorescent signal probe. The generated ascorbic acid can etch MnO2 nanosheet to control the concentration of Na2SO3, and the latter can convert 9-anthraldehyde into sulfuretted anthracene accompanied by a fluorescent color transition from green to blue against fenitrothion. The practicality and accuracy of these two immunosensors were further validated by standard GC-MS/MS.

Fenitrothion and other organophosphorus pesticide standards were obtained from Tanmo standard center (Beijing, China). The ascorbic acid, ascorbyl-2-phosphate, 9-anthraldehyde, and N,N-dimethylformamide were supplied from Yuanye Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). K3[Fe(CN)6], FeCl3, KMnO4, sodium dodecyl sulfate, and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride (TMB) were purchased from Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium sulfite and Tween-20 were supplied from Damao Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The VHHjd8 and coating antigen was prepared from our previous study [22] and VHHjd8-ALP was constructed using pET22b-ALP as the fusion expression vector [21].

Absorbance and fluorescence were measured using a SpectraMax i3X microplate reader (Molecular Devices, USA). The 96-well microplates were washed in a Wellwash™ microplate washer (Thermo Fisher, Hudson, NH, USA). High-speed centrifugation (SORVALL LYNX 4000) and ultraviolet-visible spectrum (NanoDrop 2000 C) were purchased from Thermo Fisher (Shanghai, China).

The following buffers and solutions were used in this study: (1) Tris buffer: 10 mM, pH 7.4; (2) Washing buffer: Tris buffer with 0.05% Tween-20; (3) ALP buffer: 5 mM Tris, pH 9.5, with 2 mM MgCl2; (4) Acetate buffer: 100 mM, pH 4.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression and purification of VHHjd8-ALP fusion protein
ce:section.ce:para: The VHHjd8 sequence was amplified from VHHjd8-pComb3xss by forward and reverse primers for vector construction (Table S1). The vector construction and preparation of VHHjd8-ALP fusion protein were referred to our previous work [21] (see Supporting Information).

The MnO2 nanosheet was synthesized according to Dong et al. [23]. Briefly, sodium dodecyl sulfate (461.4 mg) and H2SO4 (45 μL, 1.8 M) were mixed with 50 mL distilled water and stirred at 95 °C for 15 min. Afterward, 1.6 mL of KMnO4 (0.05 M) was added for one-hour reaction at 85 °C to yield a dark brown precipitate. The product was washed with H2O and ethyl alcohol for three times. Finally, the obtained MnO2 nanosheet was dispersed within an ultrasonic bath for 30 min and stored at 4 °C.

VHHjd8-ALP, fenitrothion standard, and sample extraction were diluted by Tris buffer for indirect competitive immunoassay. A series concentration of fenitrothion or sample extraction was added as competitor to the antigen-coated 96-well microplate (50 μL/well), and the diluted VHHjd8-ALP was subsequently added (50 μL/well) for 40 min incubation at 37 °C. Afterward, the plate was washed five times with washing buffer. The AAP (5 mM, in ALP buffer) was added (100 μL/well) for 40 min incubation at 37 °C. Afterward, different substrates were added for MRFIA and MRVIA development, respectively.

For MRVIA, K3[Fe(CN)6] (2 mM, in acetate buffer) was added (50 μL/well) for 5-min reaction. Afterward, FeCl3 (2 mM, in acetate buffer) was added (50 μL/well) for another 5-min reaction. Then the absorbance at 708 nm (A708) and 426 nm (A426) were measured, respectively. The calibration curve was fitted using OriginPro2022 software with the value of A708/A426 against the concentration of fenitrothion.

For MRFIA, 25 μL of MnO2 nanosheet (0.0625 mg/mL, in acetate buffer) was added to each well and shaken for 5 min. Afterward, 25 μL of Na2SO3 (2.5 mM, in acetate buffer) was added for 5-min shaking, and 25 μL of 9-anthraldehyde (0.1 mM, in 1% N,N-dimethylformamide solution) was subsequently added for 5-min reaction. The emission of 9-anthraldehyde (520 nm, I520) of the wells in the absence of fenitrothion was the interreference. The sulfuretted anthracene (420 nm, I420) was measured in the presence of certain concentration of fenitrothion. Both I520 and I420 were measured with the excitation of 365 nm. The calibration curve was fitted using OriginPro2022 software with the I420/I520 value against the concentration of fenitrothion.

Three levels of fenitrothion (0.1, 0.3, and 1.0 mg/kg) were added to 10 g of samples (apple, cucumber, and Chinese cabbage), respectively. The samples were extracted by acetonitrile and purified with anhydrous MgSO4 and primary secondary amine. The details of sample extraction are summarized in the Supporting Information. The spiked sample extraction was filtrated by 0.22 µm membrane for GC-MS/MS analysis and 10-fold diluted for MRFIA and MRVIA analysis. The parameters of GC-MS/MS were summarized in Table S2
.

In our previous study, the nanobody namely VHHjd8 showed high sensitivity and specificity (Table S3) against fenitrothion was prepared [22]. To prepared a bifunctional protein showed both antigen binding ability and signal generation, the VHHjd8 was fused with ALP via genetic sequence construction. The SDS-PAGE showed the successful construction of VHHjd8-ALP (Fig. S1), which was then used for subsequent studies.

The scheme of MRVIA was showed in Fig. 1. In the presence of VHHjd8-ALP, the generated ascorbic acid converted K3[Fe(CN)6] into K4[Fe(CN)6], and the latter reacted with Fe3+ to form Prussian blue via self-assembly, otherwise the AAP-Fe3+ was generated [20,24]. For Prussian blue particle characterization, the transmission electron microscope image ( Fig. 2A) shows the cubic shape and the ferrum element uniformly distributed in Prussian blue (Fig. 2B and C). The energy dispersive spectrometer spectrum further confirmed the Prussian blue element construction (Fig. 2D), indicating the successful generation of Prussian blue. To verify the feasibility of MRVIA, the color reactions were investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 2E. The Fe3+ showed no absorbance disturbance ranged from 400 nm to 800 nm (Fig. 2E, line a) and no reactivity to K3[Fe(CN)6] (Fig. 2E, line c). In contrast, when Fe3+ was mixed with K4[Fe(CN)6], Prussian blue was formed via self-assembly (Fig. 2E, line e). Although both K3[Fe(CN)6] and AAP-Fe3+ complex have an absorption peak at 426 nm (Fig. 2E, lines b and d), the photograph of Fig. 2E shows significant visible color resolution. Orange is the feature color of the AAP-Fe3+ complex, which can cover up the color interference of K3[Fe(CN)6]. In the presence of VHHjd8-ALP and AAP, K3[Fe(CN)6] was reduced into K4[Fe(CN)6] by generated ascorbic acid and a remarkable absorption peak of Prussian blue was observed (Fig. 2E, line f). In summary, the conversion from AAP-Fe3+ to Prussian blue can be triggered by VHHjd8-ALP, which resulted in a color transition from orange to blue. Therefore, the MRVIA can be developed accordingly.

The VHHjd8-ALP was used to sense fenitrothion for the development of MRVIA. As the concentration of fenitrothion decreased, the percentage of Prussian blue was increased with a serial color transition from orange to blue ( Fig. 3A). A MRVIA calibration curve of A708/A426 against fenitrothion was obtained (Fig. 3B and C) after optimization of coating antigen concentration (Table S4). The MRVIA calibration curve showed the IC50 value of 6.7 ng/mL and the limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 ng/mL, with a linear range from 4.7 to 11.6 ng/mL (R2 =0.997). Although the proposed MRVIA showed no significant sensitivity and time (approximately 90–100 min) improvement compared with the conventional icELISA [22] (
Table 3), the recognizable color transition of MRVIA can be read by naked-eye, which avoided the using of reader and can be used for on-site monitoring.

To further improve the sensitivity of multicolor assays, we employed 9-anthraldehyde as fluorescent signal probe to propose a MRFIA based on VHHjd8-ALP. In the presence of Na2SO3, 9-anthraldehyde was converted into sulfuretted anthracene [25]. With the aid of handheld ultraviolet equipment, an obvious fluorescence color transition from green to blue can be observed by naked eye ( Fig. 4A). The spectral characterization showed an emission shift from 520 nm to 420 nm caused by the conversion (Fig. 4B). Based on the above mechanism, we further proposed the MRFIA (Fig. 4C). The added MnO2 nanosheet can control the concentration of Na2SO3 via oxidation. After one-step indirect competitive immunoassay, the VHHjd8-ALP generated ascorbic acid which can etch MnO2 to regulate the final fluorescent color transition.

MnO2 has rich oxidation states, and its excellent oxidizing ability and sensitive response to ascorbic acid have been well studied [26–28]. The MnO2 nanosheet can be dissolved in aqueous solution evenly, showing deep brown color and an absorption peak at 425 nm (Fig. S2A). Transmission electron microscope image shows thin and wrinkled layer shape of the MnO2 nanosheet (Fig. S2B), providing a great specific surface area for the high-efficiency activity to Na2SO3 and ascorbic acid. Furthermore, TMB was used as the substrate to investigate the oxidative activity of MnO2 in a serial of pH solution. As a result, MnO2 nanosheet exhibited the highest oxidative activity at pH 4.0 (Fig. S2C), which was chosen as the working pH value.

The pH value for emission of 9-anthraldehyde and sulfuretted anthracene were further optimized. The fluorescence of 9-anthraldehyde was not obviously affected by pH value, while sulfuretted anthracene exhibited the highest fluorescent intensity at pH 4.0 ( Fig. 5A and B). Moreover, at acid condition (pH 4.0), the oxidative activity of MnO2 can be improved significantly. Based on the optimized pH condition, the concentration of Na2SO3 was further optimized. Fig. 5C shows the obvious fluorescence color transition at the 2.5 mM of Na2SO3. Afterward, the concentration of the MnO2 nanosheet was also optimized. Na2SO3 was completely oxidized at the present of 0.0625 mg/mL of MnO2 and low concentration of MnO2 showed no color influence on the fluorescence of 9-anthraldehyde or sulfuretted anthracene (Fig. 5D). The optimized parameters were used for the development of MRFIA subsequently.

Based on the above optimized parameters, the concentration of coating antigen and antibody was further optimized to develop MRFIA. Results showed that the optimal concentration of the coating antigen and VHHjd8-ALP was 2 μg/mL and 1.75 μg/mL, respectively (Table S5). Afterward, the optimized MRFIA was used for the color sensing of fenitrothion ( Fig. 6A). As fenitrothion decreased, the 9-anthraldehyde was converted into sulfuretted anthracene, accompanied by a visible fluorescent color transition from green to blue. To be noticed, the spectrum of MRFIA showed that a non-negligible increase for I520 was observed as I420 increased (Fig. 6B). Therefore, the constant I520 of blank control was selected as inter reference. The MRFIA calibration curve of I420/I520 against fenitrothion showed the IC50 value was 6.2 ng/mL with the LOD of 1.3 ng/mL and the quantitative range from 2.6 to 19.5 ng/mL, with a good linearity (R2 =0.994) (Fig. 6C). Although the developed MRFIA required more steps for analysis, the detection time (approximately 95–105 min) was closed to that of MRVIA. But the sensitivity of MRFIA was 2.3-fold improved in comparison with that of MRVIA and icELISA (Table 3). By using handheld ultraviolet equipment, a visible fluorescent color transition can be easily observed by the naked eye. The developed MRFIA requires no bulky readers, which was convenient and economical.

The accuracy and practicability of MRVIA and MRFIA were assessed by a recovery test, and the results were evaluated by standard GC-MS/MS. For the matrix effect test, samples were diluted to four levels (0-fold, 5-fold, 10-fold, and 20-fold). The matrix interference effect was removed until 10-fold diluted (Fig. S3). Table 1 shows that the average recoveries of 87.5–109.7% and 82.1–109.9% were observed for the MRFIA and MRVIA, with a coefficient of variances (CVs) ranging from 5.0% to 10.7% and 2.4–10.1%, respectively. The average recoveries obtained by GC-MS/MS ranged from 79.9% to 100%, with CVs between 0.3% and 5.4%. The real sample test was further employed to evaluate the accuracy of the two immunosensors. The results showed good agreement with GC-MS/MS (Table 2). All the above results showed that the accuracy of the MRVIA and MRFIA was comparable for rapid screening of food samples. Moreover, the two developed immunosensors are more time-saving. For MRVIA and MRFIA, each microplate can achieve simultaneous detection for 24–32 samples and only require an average of 100 min. In contrast, according to the sample-by-sample detection and long period (approximately 24 min) for each sample [29], GC-MS/MS takes approximately10∼13 h to detect the same number of samples. Consequently, the proposed MRVIA and MRFIA are promising for screen purpose.

Table 3 summarizes the relevant reported immunosensors for fenitrothion. The LOD values of the developed MRFIA and MRVIA were much lower than those of most methods in buffer system. However, the food sample matrix limited the sensitivity of the two immunosensors. Sample extractions were need to be diluted 10-fold before analysis, which caused the LOD to increase to 13 ng/mL and 30 ng/mL for MRFIA and MRVIA, respectively. Nevertheless, the LODs were still lower than the MRLs set by China [3], USA [5], and FAO [6], which can be used for the real sample analysis. Furthermore, compared with the other one-color sensors, the developed multicolor immunosensors showed the advantages for naked-eye semiquantitative analysis without bulky readers, which was simple, rapid, and economical.

In this study, an anti-fenitrothion nanobody-alkaline phosphatase fusion protein was employed to construct a dual readout visible immunosensors, MRVIA and MRFIA. For the development of MRVIA, K3[Fe(CN)6] and Fe3+ were used for the color response of ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) and ascorbic acid. As the AAP was converted to ascorbic acid, it can be observed that a color transition from orange to blue against fenitrothion caused by a ratiometric change from AAP-Fe3+ complex to Prussian blue. The MRFIA further improve the sensor sensitivity owing to the high response of ascorbic acid to MnO2, which can regulate the conversion from 9-anthraldehyde to sulfuretted anthracene and thereby showed a color transition from green to blue for sensing fenitrothion. The two developed immunosensors achieved multicolor sensing relied on the ratiometric change of two constant wavelength signals (absorbance or fluorescence), which was more economical, straightforward, and faster than that of the reported precious-metal-based multicolor immunosensors. The recovery and blind samples test verified the accuracy and practicability of MRFIA and MRVIA, which were promising for rapid quantitative or semiquantitative screening of fenitrothion in bulk food samples.

Min-Ling Liu: Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xi Zeng: Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hao Deng: Validation. Yu Wang: Validation. Yi-Feng Zhang: Methodology. Methodology. Yu-Dong Shen: Methodology. Lin Luo:Hong Wang: Methodology. Zi-Jian Chen: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Zhen-Lin Xu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development of China (2019YFE0116600), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation, China (2021A1515110513), the Science and Technology Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (2018A050506072) and the Key Project of the Guangdong Provincial High School, China (2019KJDXM002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132734.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical methodology for trace determination of propoxur and fenitrothion pesticide residues by decanoic acid modified magnetic nanoparticles
Molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.G. Canli, B. Surucu, H.I. Ulusoy, E. Yilmaz, A. Kabir, M. Locatelli, Analytical methodology for trace determination of propoxur and fenitrothion pesticide residues by decanoic acid modified magnetic nanoparticles, Molecules 24 (2019) 4621, https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules24244621.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of haptens of organophosphorus pesticides and development of immunoassays for fenitrothion
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.A. Cho, J. Seok, H. Lee, Y.J. Kim, Y.C. Park, Y.T. Lee, Synthesis of haptens of organophosphorus pesticides and development of immunoassays for fenitrothion, Anal. Chim. Acta 522 (2004) 215–222, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2004.05.083.

Analyst

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhen-Lin Xu received his Ph.D. degree in Food Science at South China Agricultural University in 2011. After that he served as a post-doctor fellow at South China Botanical Garden, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He joined South China Agricultural University in 2013 and is now a Full professor. From 2015–2016, Dr. Xu worked with Prof. Bruce Hammock at the University of California Davis as a visiting professor. His research interests mainly focus on the production of all kinds of antibodies and development of immunoassays for small molecules.

Zi-Jian Chen has a Ph.D. (2020) in food science from South China Agricultural University. Dr. Chen is a post-doctor working for food safety at the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety. Today, he focuses on rapidly analyzing hazardous compounds in foods, including monoclonal antibody and genetically engineered antibody production, nanozyme preparation and characterization, and immunosensor development.
